revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]][[no:Begreper i japansk buddhisme]] {{Short description|none}} {{Dynamic list}} {{JapaneseBuddhism}}
This is the '''glossary of Japanese Buddhism''', including major terms the casual (or brand-new) reader might find useful in understanding articles on the subject. Words followed by an asterisk (*) are illustrated by an image in one of the photo galleries. Within definitions, words set in '''boldface''' are defined elsewhere in the glossary.
__NOTOC__ {{compact TOC|side=yes|top=yes|num=yes}}
==A== * ''agyō''* (阿形) – A type of statue (of a '''[[#N|Niō]]''', '''[[#K|komainu]]''', etc.) with its mouth open to pronounce the sound "a", first letter of the {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} alphabet and symbol of the beginning of all things.<ref name="jaanus"/> See also ''ungyō''. * Amida '''Nyorai''' (阿弥陀如来) – Japanese name of [[Amitābha]], deity worshiped mainly by the [[:en:Pure Land sect|Pure Land sect]].<ref name="kojien">''{{ill|Kōjien|en|Kōjien|qid=Q1192611|lt=Kōjien|1=Kōjien}} Japanese dictionary''</ref> * {{nihongo|''Anjitsu''/''Anshitsu''|, lit. "Hermitage Residence"}} – A {{ill|Hermitage (religious retreat)|en|Hermitage (religious retreat)|qid=Q513550|lt=Hermitage|1=Hermitage (religious retreat)}}. * arhat – see '''arakan'''. * ''arakan*'' (阿羅漢) – the highest level of Buddhist ascetic practice, or someone who has reached it.<ref name="kojien"/> The term is often shortened to just ''rakan'' (羅漢).
==B== *bay – see '''[[#K|ken]]'''. *''{{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|qid=Q2900274|lt=Bettō|1=Bettō}}'' (別当) – Previously the title of the head of powerful temples, e.g. Tōdai-ji, [[Kōfuku-ji]], etc. (still in use at the former). Also a monk who was present at {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} to perform Buddhist rites until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}, when the government forbade with the '''[[shinbutsu bunri]]''' policy the mixing of Shinto and {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}.<ref name="kojien"/> *{{ill|Bodhi|en|Bodhi|qid=Q502029|lt=bodai|1=Bodhi}} – from the [[:en:Pāli|Pāli]] and {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} word for ''way'' or knowledge.<ref name="kojien"/> **'''satori''', or Buddhist enlightenment.<ref name="kojien"/> **Ceremonies and other efforts to ensure someone's happiness in the next world, after death.<ref name="kojien"/> *{{ill|Bodaiji|en|Bodaiji|qid=Q1084697|lt=Bodaiji|1=Bodaiji}} – lit. "'''{{ill|Bodhi|en|Bodhi|qid=Q502029|lt=Bodhi|1=Bodhi}}''' temple". A temple which, generation after generation, takes care of a family's dead giving them burial and performing ceremonies in their favor.<ref name="kojien"/> See for example the Tokugawa's [[Kan'ei-ji]].<ref name="kojien"/> *''Bon'' (盆) – See {{ill|Obon|en|Obon|qid=Q1154888|lt=Obon|1=Obon}} *''{{ill|Bosatsu|en|Bosatsu|qid=Q23925162|lt=Bosatsu|1=Bosatsu}}'' (菩薩) **A '''{{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}}''' **The historical Gautama Buddha, before enlightenment.<ref name="kojien"/> **In {{ill|Mahayana|en|Mahayana|qid=Q48362|lt=Mahayana|1=Mahayana}}, someone who could enter paradise but chooses not to, to help others achieve enlightenment. **Someone who is in pursuit of ''satori''.<ref name="kojien"/> **during the '''[[shinbutsu-shūgō]]''' period, an honorific used for Japanese '''kami''', as for example in "[[Hachiman]] Bosatsu".<ref name="kojien"/> {{wiktionary|Buddha}} *[[:en:Gautama Buddha|Buddha]] – the term Buddha in the upper case can refer to: **Shakyamuni Tathāgata Buddha, Indian spiritual and philosophical teacher and founder of Buddhism; Gautama Buddha. **one who has become enlightened (i.e., awakened to the truth, or ''{{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|qid=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}}'') **Any of the other Buddhas named in Buddhist scriptures. **A statue or image of any Buddha. *''buddha'' – the term 'buddha' in the lower case refers not to Gautama Buddha but to: **a statue of Gautama Buddha **any of the other {{ill|Buddhahood|en|Buddhahood|qid=Q56247062|lt=buddha|1=Buddhahood}}s (enlightened ones) named in Buddhist scriptures. *Buddha's footprints – see ''bussokuseki'' *''{{ill|Buddha footprint|en|Buddha footprint|qid=Q583098|lt=bussokuseki|1=Buddha footprint}}*'' (仏足石) – lit. Buddha's foot (print) stone. A stone carved with footprints representing Buddha.<ref name="kojien"/> Before the instruction of the human figure, Buddha was represented only indirectly through his footprints. *''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}'' or ''Butsu-dō*'' (仏殿・仏堂) – lit. "Hall of Buddha". **A '''Zen''' temple's '''main hall'''. Seems to have two stories, but has in fact only one and measures either 3x3 or 5x5 bays. **Any building enshrining the statue of Buddha or of a '''bodhisattva''' and dedicated to prayer.<ref name="kojien"/> *''{{ill|Butsudan|en|Butsudan|qid=Q327521|lt=Butsudan|1=Butsudan}}*'' (仏壇) – a tabernacle used in homes to install Buddhist images and [[:en:Ihai|tablets]] recording the posthumous names of deceased family members.<ref name="kojien"/> *''buppō'' (仏法) – see '''hō''' *''[[Japanese pagoda|buttō]]'' (仏塔) – a '''stupa''' or one of its relatives.<ref name="kojien"/> See also '''tō''', '''pagoda''', '''gorintō''', '''hōkyōintō''', '''sotoba''', '''sekitō''' and '''tahōtō'''.
==C== *''chinju'' (鎮守/鎮主) – the tutelary '''kami''' or tutelary '''shrine''' of a certain area or {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}.<ref name="kojien"/> *''{{ill|Chinjusha|en|Chinjusha|qid=Q13479654|lt=Chinjusha|1=Chinjusha}}'''''*''' (鎮守社/鎮主社) – a small '''shrine''' built at a Buddhist '''temple''' and dedicated to its tutelary kami.<ref name="kojien"/> *''chōzuya'' (手水舎) – see '''temizuya'''. *''chūmon*'' (中門) – in a temple, the gate after the '''nandaimon''' connected to a '''kairō'''.<ref name="kojien"/> See also '''mon'''.
==D== *Daihi Kannon (大悲観音) -See '''Senju Kannon'''. *{{ill|Five Tathāgatas|en|Five Tathāgatas|qid=Q265911|lt=Dainichi|1=Five Tathāgatas}} '''Nyorai''' (大日如来) – Japanese name of {{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Vairocana|1=Vairocana}}, of which the Japanese ''kami'' [[Amaterasu]] is considered an emanation (see '''honji suijaku'''). *''[[danka system|danka]]'' (檀家) – a family or individual affiliated to a particular temple is called one of its ''danka''. See also '''danka system''' *[[danka system]] (檀家制度 danka-seido) – a system in which a family (the '''danka''') contributes to the support of a particular Buddhist temple, which in return provides its services.<ref name="inj">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> This kind of temple affiliation became mandatory during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, when was used by the shogunate for political ends (see also '''terauke'''). *-''dō'' (堂) – Lit. ''hall''. Suffix for the name of the buildings part of a temple. The prefix can be the name of a deity associated with it (e.g. '''Yakushi-dō''', or ''Yakushi'' hall) or express the building's function within the temple's compound (e.g. '''hon-dō''', or main hall).<ref>Hall names are capitalized only when they refer to specific examples (e.g. XX-ji's Main Hall) or include proper names of deities (e.g. Yakushi-dō).</ref> See also '''Butsu-dō''', '''hō-dō''', '''hon-dō''', '''jiki-dō''', '''kaisan-dō''', '''kō-dō''', '''kon-dō''', '''kyō-dō''', '''mandara-dō''', '''miei-dō''', '''mi-dō''', '''sō-dō''', '''Yakushi-dō''' and '''zen-dō'''.
==E== *''[[:en:Enma|Enma]]*'', ''Emmaten'' or ''Emmaō'' (閻魔, 閻魔天 or 閻魔王) – Japanese transliteration of {{ill|Yama|en|Yama|qid=Q45097|lt=Yama|1=Yama}}, the ruler of the underworld in Buddhist mythology.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> *''enlightenment'' – see '''satori'''.
==F== *family temple – see '''bodaiji'''. *funeral temple – see '''bodaiji'''. *''Fuju-fuse-gi'' (不受不施義) – the duty of a {{ill|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q311478en|Nichiren|1=Nichiren|2=ja|3=日蓮|4=ko|5=니치렌|6=de|7=Nichiren|8=es|9=Nichiren|10=fr|11=Nichiren|12=it|13=Nichiren|14=nl|15=Nichiren|16=no|17=Nichiren|lt=Nichiren|qid=Q311478qid=Q311478|lt=Nichiren}} sect member not to accept anything from, or give anything to a non-believer. *{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|qid=Q44372|lt=Five Mountain System|1=Five Mountain System}} – See '''Gozan Seido'''.
==G== * ''garan'' – see '''shichi-dō garan'''. * ''gejin'' (外陣) – the portion of a '''hon-dō''' (main hall) open to the public, as opposed to the '''naijin''', reserved to the deity.<ref name="jaanus"/> * {{nihongo|''Gokō''|, lit. "Background Light"}} – The ''Shinto-Buddhist'' equivalent of an ''[[:en:Aureole|Aureole]]'' or ''{{ill|Halo (religious iconography)|en|Halo (religious iconography)|qid=Q3341893|lt=Halo|1=Halo (religious iconography)}}''. * ''[[Homa (ritual)|Goma]]'' (護摩) – a ritual involving making offerings into a consecrated fire. Initiated mostly by the {{ill|Shingon Buddhism|en|Shingon Buddhism|qid=Q550182|lt=Shingon|1=Shingon Buddhism}} sect. * ''{{ill|Five Mountain System|en|Five Mountain System|qid=Q44372|lt=Gozan Seido|1=Five Mountain System}}'' (五山制度) – A nationwide network of '''Zen''' temples, called Five Mountain system or Five Mountains in English, with at its top five temples in Kamakura (the ''Kamakura Gozan'') and five in Kyoto (the Kyoto Gozan), which during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}} was a ''de facto'' part of the government's infrastructure, helping rule the country.<ref name="inj"/> * ''[[gongen]]'' (権現) ** A Buddhist god that chooses to appear as a Japanese ''kami'' in order to take the Japanese to spiritual salvation.<ref name="SoS">Encyclopedia of Shinto, [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_g.html#gongen ''Gongen''] accessed on October 5, 2008</ref> ** Name sometimes used for '''shrine'''s (e.g. "Tokusō Gongen") before the '''shinbutsu bunri'''. * ''[[gorintō]]''* (五輪塔) – a type of '''stupa''' common in Buddhist temples and cemeteries consisting of five shapes (a cube, a sphere, a pyramid, a crescent and a lotus flower) one on top of the other representing the {{ill|Godai (Japanese philosophy)|en|Godai (Japanese philosophy)|qid=Q3109675|lt=five elements|1=Godai (Japanese philosophy)}} of Buddhist cosmology.<ref name="jaanus"/>
== Gallery: A to G == <gallery> File:Nikko Toshogu Nio M3042.jpg|An example of ''agyō'' (in this case, a '''Niō''') File:Hokaiji01s1024.jpg|{{ill|Hōkai-ji|ja|3=法界寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkai-ji|qq=}}'s ''Amida-dō'' File:Kitain-2042.jpg|A statue of an ''arakan'' at {{ill|Kita-in|en|Kita-in|qid=Q3080561|lt=Kita-in|1=Kita-in}} File:Japanese_Buddhist_altar_001.jpg|A ''butsudan'' File:Hasedera Kamakura 20080523.jpg|A ''bussokuseki''
File:Myoshinji-M9719.jpg|[[Myōshin-ji]]'s ''Butsuden'' File:Motoyamaji-Chinjyudou.jpg|{{ill|Motoyama-ji|en|Motoyama-ji|qid=Q3325310|lt=Motoyama-ji|1=Motoyama-ji}}'s ''chinjū-dō'' File:Shinbutsu-Shugo-at-Komyoji.jpg|{{ill|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|en|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|qid=Q3076914|lt=Kōmyō-ji|1=Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)}}'s ''chinjūsha'' File:Horyu-ji03s3200.jpg|''Chūmon'' at [[Hōryū-ji]] File:Saimyoji Temple 5.jpg|A statue of ''Enma'' File:Mimizuka-M1773 corrected.jpg|A ''gorintō'' </gallery>
==H== *''[[haibutsu kishaku]]'' **A current of thought, continuous in Japan's history, advocating the expulsion of Buddhism from Japan.<ref name="EOS.HK">[http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=1354 Encyclopedia of Shinto – Haibutsu Kishaku] accessed on March 15, 2008</ref> **A wave of anti-Buddhist violence that hit Japan in 1868 after the forcible separation of Buddhism and Shinto ('''shinbutsu bunri''').<ref name="EOS.HK"/> *''hall'' – see -'''dō''' *''[[Honji suijaku#Inverted honji suijaku|han-honji suijaku]]'' (反本地垂迹) – a theory which in the 14th century reversed the '''honji suijaku''' theory, claiming ''kami'' were superior to Buddhist gods. *''hattō*'' (法堂) – lit. "'''Dharma''' hall". A building dedicated to lectures by the chief priest on Buddhism's scriptures (the '''hō''').<ref name="kojien"/> *''hō'' (法) – the teachings of Buddha ({{ill|Dharma|en|Dharma|qid=Q134293|lt=Dharma|1=Dharma}}) and the '''sūtra'''s.<ref name="kojien"/> Also called '''buppō'''. *''hō-dō'' (法堂) – see '''hattō'''. *''hōjō*'' (方丈) – the living quarters of the head priest of a Zen temple.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''Hokekyō'' or ''Hokkekyō'' (法華経) – the {{ill|en:Lotus Sutra|en|en:Lotus Sutra|qid=Q861212|lt=en:Lotus Sutra|1=Lotus Sutra}}, one of the Buddhist sacred scriptures. *''Hokke-dō*'' (法華堂) – lit. "Lotus Sūtra hall". In {{ill|Tendai|en|Tendai|qid=Q855964|lt=Tendai|1=Tendai}} Buddhism, a hall whose layout allows walking around a statue for meditation.<ref name="jaanus"/> The purpose of walking is to concentrate on the '''Hokekyō''' and seek the ultimate truth.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''hōkyō-zukuri*'' (宝形造) – a type of roof consisting of four or more sloping, curved triangles meeting at the top. An example is Hōryū-ji's Yume-dono. *''{{ill|Hōkyōintō|en|Hōkyōintō|qid=Q3146365|lt=Hōkyōintō|1=Hōkyōintō}}*'' (宝篋印塔) – a tower-shaped variant of a '''stupa''', so called because it originally contained the Hōkyōin Dharani '''sutra'''.<ref name="jaanus"/> See also '''stupa'''. *''hōmyō'' (法名) – religious name received after votes (Buddhist name) or posthumous name given to a deceased person.<ref name="kojien"/> *''honbō*'' (本坊) – residence of the '''jushoku''', or head priest, of a temple.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''[[:en:Hon-dō|Hon-dō]]*'' (本堂) – Lit. "main hall", it is the building that houses the most important statues and objects of cult.<ref name="jaanus"/> The term is thought to have evolved to avoid the term '''kon-dō''' used by six Nara sects (the ''' Nanto Rokushū''')<ref name="jaanus"/> for their main halls. Structurally similar, but less strictly defined. *''[[honji suijaku]]'' (本地垂迹) – a theory common in Japan before the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}} according to which Buddhist deities choose to appear as native ''[[kami]]'' to the Japanese in order to save them.<ref name="EOS.HK"/> *''{{ill|Hotoke|en|Hotoke|qid=Q5912431|lt=Hotoke|1=Hotoke}}'' (仏) ** Japanese term meaning for '''buddha''' (an enlightened one).<ref name="kojien"/> ** A Buddhist sacred image or statue.<ref name="kojien"/> ** A deceased person or his/her soul.<ref name="kojien"/> *''hōtō'' (宝塔) – lit. ''treasure tower''. A stone '''stupa''' constituted by a square base, a barrel-shaped body, a pyramid and a finial.<ref name="jaanus"/> Not to be confused with the similarly shaped ''[[tahōtō]]''. *''hyakudoishi*'' (百度石) – lit. "hundred times stone". Sometimes present as a point of reference for the '''hyakudomairi''' near the entrance of a '''shrine''' or {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}.<ref name="kojien"/> *''hyakudomairi'' (百度参り) – literally "a hundred visits". A worshiper with a special prayer will visit the temple a hundred times.<ref name="kojien"/> After praying, he or she must go at least back to the entrance or around a '''hyakudoishi''' for the next visit to count as a separate visit.<ref name="kojien"/>
==Gallery: H== <gallery> Image:Myoshinji-M9727.jpg|[[Myōshin-ji]]'s ''hattō'' Image:Todaiji_hokkedo.jpg|[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''Hokke-dō'' Image:Horyu-ji36s3200.jpg|An example of ''hōkyō-zukuri'': Hōryū-ji's Yume-dono Image:Mizumadera_hokyointo.jpg|A ''hōkyōintō'' File:Jingoji Kyoto Kyoto46n4592.jpg|{{ill|Jingo-ji|en|Jingo-ji|qid=Q1075034|lt=Jingo-ji|1=Jingo-ji}}'s ''honbō'' Image:TofukujiHondo.jpg|A ''hon-dō'' Image:Ashikaga Mochiujis Houtou.jpg|A ''hōtō'' Image:ShintoShrineHyakudoIshiM0872.jpg|A ''hyakudoishi'' </gallery>
==I== * {{nihongo|''Indō''|, lit. "Requiem"}} – {{ill|Requiem|en|Requiem|qid=Q51616|lt=Requiem|1=Requiem}}; ({{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}) prayers for the dead. * ''ingō'' (院号) - ** The part of a temple's full name ending in "-in" (e.g. "Toeizan Kan'ei-ji '''Endon'in'''"). The other two are the '''sangō''' and the '''jigō'''. ** The part of a '''hōmyō''' or '''kaimyō''' (posthumous names) ending in "-in".<ref name="kojien"/> * ''inzō*'' (印相) – a ''[[mudrā]]'', or Buddhist hand gesture,<ref name="kojien"/> common in Buddhist religious statues. * ''inverted honji suijaku'' – See '''han-honji suijaku'''. * [[:en:Irimoya|irimoya-zukuri]] – A {{ill|Hip roof|en|Hip roof|qid=Q162034|lt=Hip roof|1=Hip roof}} (sloping down on all four sides) integrated on two opposing sides with a {{ill|Gable|en|Gable|qid=Q1161370|lt=Gable|1=Gable}}.<ref name="jaanus"/>
==J== *''jigō'' (寺号) – a temple's main name. Often the second after the '''sangō''', and the only one in common use (e.g. "Tōeizan '''Kan'ei-ji''' Endon'in"). The last name is called the '''ingō'''. The use of the ''sangō'' came into fashion after the arrival of Zen Buddhism to Japan. *[[Jizō#In Japan|Jizō]]* (地蔵) – Ksitigarbha Bodhisattva; god guardian of children, particularly children who died before their parents, often seen wearing red votive bibs ('''yodarekake''') and hats. Parents sometimes leave at the temple a small Jizō statue in memory of their lost child. *''jiki-dō*'' (食堂) – a monastery's refectory.<ref name="jaanus"/> See also '''sai-dō'''. *[[:en:Jiriki|Jiriki]] (自力) - one's own strength *Jūni Shinnō – see Jūni Shinshō. *[[:en:Jūni Shinshō|Jūni Shinshō]]* (十二神将) – Twelve retainers who accompany '''Yakushi Nyorai'''.<ref name="kojien"/> Also known as Jūni Yakusha Taishō (十二薬叉大将) and Jūni Shinnō (十二神王). In English they are often called the Twelve Heavenly Generals.<ref>[http://www.tnm.go.jp/en/servlet/Con?processId=00&ref=&Q1=&Q2=&Q3=&Q4=&Q5=&F1=&F2=&pageId=E15&colid=C15 Standing Twelve Heavenly Generals] Tokyo National Museum, accessed on March 10, 2010</ref> *Jūni Yakusha Taishō – see Jūni Shinshō. *Jūroku {{ill|Arhat|en|Arhat|qid=Q225932|lt=Rakan|1=Arhat}} (十六羅漢) – lit. "sixteen '''arhat'''s", holy men who were at Gautama Buddha's deathbed and there were ordered by him to stay in this world to defend and maintain his teachings.<ref name="jaanus"/> They are worshiped mainly by '''Zen''' sects.<ref name="kojien"/> * jūji (住持) – see ''jūshoku''. *''jūshoku'' (住職) – The chief priest of a temple or monastery.<ref name="kojien"/>
==K== * ''[[:en:Kaimyō|Kaimyō]]'' (戒名) – see '''hōmyō'''. * ''{{ill|Kairō|en|Kairō|qid=Q7884494|lt=Kairō|1=Kairō}}*'' (回廊・廻廊) – a long and roofed {{ill|Portico|en|Portico|qid=Q185600|lt=Portico|1=Portico}}-like passage connecting two buildings.<ref name="jaanus"/> * ''[[:en:Kaisan-dō|Kaisan-dō]]'' (開山堂) – founder's hall, usually at a Zen temple. Building enshrining a statue, portrait or memorial tablet of the founder of either the temple or the sect it belongs to. Jōdo sect temples often call it '''miei-dō'''.<ref name="jaanus"/> * Kamakura Gozan – see '''Gozan Seido'''. * ''[[kami]]'' (神) – term broadly meaning "deity", but having with several separate meanings. ** deities mentioned in Japanese mythologies and local deities protecting areas, villages and families.<ref name="smyers">Smyers (1999:219)</ref> ** unnamed and non-anthropomorphic spirits found in natural phenomena.<ref name="smyers"/> ** a general sense of sacred power.<ref name="smyers"/> * [[:en:Kankiten|Kankiten]] (歓喜天) – the Buddhist version of {{ill|Hindus|en|Hindus|qid=Q10090|lt=Hindus|1=Hindus}} god [[:en:Ganesh|Ganesh]], usually represented by two elephant-headed human beings embracing each other. * [[Guan Yin|Kannon]] (観音)- the goddess of mercy and compassion, found not only in temples, but also in Shinto '''shrines'''. * ''[[karamon]]'' (唐門) – generic term for a gate with an arched roof.<ref name="jaanus"/> See also '''mon'''. * ''[[:en:Karesansui|Karesansui]]*'' (枯山水) – lit. ''dry landscape''. A Japanese rock garden, often present in Zen temples, and sometimes found in temples of other sects too. * ''[[katōmado]]*'' (華頭窓) – a bell shaped window originally developed at Zen temples in China, but widely used by other Buddhist sects as well as in lay buildings. * '''{{nihongo3|{{lit|Fastened-off World}}||[[Kekkai (Buddhism)|Kekkai]]}}''' – a restriction in access to a specific area, barred-off often for the purposes of training &/or purification in esoteric Buddhism (and Hinduism). * ''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|qid=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}*'' (間) ** counter for the spaces between pillars (translated as '''bay''' in English).<ref name="kojien"/> A first Rank '''sanmon''' is for example five ''ken'' across. ** unit of measurement equivalent to 197 cm until around 1650, when it was shortened to 181.8 to indirectly increase land taxes.<ref name="kojien"/> * ''keshin'' (化身) – a personification of a deity, an {{ill|Avatar|en|Avatar|qid=Q83090|lt=Avatar|1=Avatar}}.<ref name="kojien"/> * ''{{ill|Gable|en|Gable|qid=Q1161370|lt=kirizuma-zukuri|1=Gable}}'' (切妻造) – a roof style involving the use of two gables ({{ill|Gable|en|Gable|qid=Q1161370|lt=gabled roof|1=Gable}}). * ''kō-dō*'' (講堂) – lecture hall of a non-Zen '''garan'''.<ref name="kojien"/> * ''{{ill|Provincial temple|en|Provincial temple|qid=Q1207757|lt=kokubun-ji|1=Provincial temple}}'' – provincial temples ({{nihongo||国分寺|kokubun-ji}} for monks; {{nihongo||国分尼寺|kokubun-niji}} for nuns) established by {{ill|Emperor Shōmu|en|Emperor Shōmu|qid=Q349263|lt=Emperor Shōmu|1=Emperor Shōmu}} in each province of Japan. Tōdai-ji served as the head of all the ''kokubun-ji'' and {{ill|Hokke-ji|en|Hokke-ji|qid=Q3076936|lt=Hokke-ji|1=Hokke-ji}} of the ''kokubun-niji''. * ''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|qid=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}*'' (金堂) – lit. "golden hall", it is the main hall of a '''garan''', housing the main object of worship.<ref name="jaanus"/> Unlike a '''butsuden''', it is a true two-story building (although the second story may sometimes be missing) measuring 9x7 bays.<ref name="jaanus"/> * {{ill|Komainu|en|Komainu|qid=Q20155846|lt=Komainu|1=Komainu}} (狛犬) – lit. "Korean dogs". Lion-like figures placed at the entrance of a temple or shrine to ward off evils spirits.<ref name="jaanus"/> Called "lion-dogs" in English. * ''[[:en:Kongōrikishi|Kongōrikishi]]*'' (金剛力士) – see '''Niō'''. *Korean dogs – See ''komainu''. * ''korō'' or ''kurō'' (鼓楼) – tower housing a drum that marks the passing of time. It used to face the ''shōrō'' and lie next to the '''kō-dō''', but now the drum is usually kept in the '''rōmon'''.<ref name="kojien"/> * {{nihongo3|{{lit|Nine Hand Seals}}||[[Kuji-in#Meanings in Shinto|Kuji-in]]}} – A system of ''{{ill|Mudra|en|Mudra|qid=Q208030|lt=Mudra|1=Mudra}}'' and associated ''{{ill|Mantra|en|Mantra|qid=Q131510|lt=Mantra|1=Mantra}}'' that consist of nine syllables. * {{nihongo3|{{lit|Nine Symbolic Cuts}}||{{ill|Kuji-kiri|en|Kuji-kiri|qid=Q1014383|lt=Kuji-kiri|1=Kuji-kiri}}}} – A system of ''mudras'' and associated ''mantras'' that consist of nine syllables, based on ''Kuji-in''. * ''kuin*'' (庫院) – kitchen/office of a Zen '''garan'''. A building hosting the galleys, the kitchen, and the offices of a temple.<ref name="kojien"/> Usually situated in front and to the side of the '''butsuden''', facing the '''sō-dō'''. Also called '''kuri'''. * ''kuri'' (庫裏) – see ''kuin'' * ''kyō-dō'' (経堂) – see '''[[kyōzō]]'''. * Kyoto Gozan – see '''Gozan Seido'''. * ''[[kyōzō]]'' (経蔵) – lit. "scriptures deposit". Repository of {{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|qid=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}}s and books about the temple's history.<ref name="jaanus"/> Also called '''kyō–dō'''.
==Gallery: I to K== <gallery> File:Mudra_inzou.jpg|Buddha making an ''inzō'' File:Nenbutsu-ji jizo and Tanuki.jpg|A ''jizō'' File:Engyoji17s4592.jpg|[[Engyō-ji]]'s ''jiki-dō'' File:Kamakura Hasedera Jizo.jpg|A ''jizō'' with a votive bib and a woolen hat File:Tairyuji10s4592.jpg|''Jizō'', each representing a lost child File:Tijinoyakusi2.JPG|Yakushi flanked by one of the Jūni Shinshō File:Lightmatter Hsi Lai Temple Arhat Garden.jpg|The Jūroku Rakan, or Sixteen Arhats File:薬師寺回廊.jpg|A ''kairō'' File:TofukujiKaisando.jpg|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''kaisan-dō'' File:Daienin Kannon.JPG|Kannon, goddess of mercy File:Hogonji00bs3872.jpg|{{ill|Hōgon-ji|en|Hōgon-ji|qid=Q3080736|lt=Hōgon-ji|1=Hōgon-ji}}'s ''karamon'' File:Katoumado.jpg|a ''katōmado'' File:Shitennoj honbo garden06s3200.jpg|[[Shitennō-ji]]'s ''karesansui'' File:Cherry-Blossoms-Dankazura-Kamakura.jpg|A pair of ''komainu'', one '''agyō''', one '''ungyō''' File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|Tōfuku-ji's ''sanmon'' is 5 ''ken'' wide. File:Kyoto Toji Kodo C0929.jpg|''Kō-dō'' at [[Tō-ji]] File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref01s5s4290.jpg|''Kon-dō'' at {{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|qid=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji|1=Tōshōdai-ji}} </gallery>
==M== *main hall – the building of a temple housing the most important object of worship. See ''Butsuden or Butsu-dō'', ''hon-dō'' and ''kon-dō''. Each type possesses specific structural characteristics. *''[[mandala|mandara]]'' (曼陀羅) – a mandala, or diagram that contains Buddhist images and illustrates Buddhist cosmology.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''mandara-dō'' (曼荼羅堂) – lit. "hall of mandalas", but the name is presently used only for Taimadera's Main Hall in Nara.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''manji''* (卍）- the Japanese name of the {{ill|Swastika|en|Swastika|qid=Q45513|lt=Swastika|1=Swastika}}, symbol used for Buddhist temples in Japanese maps. *{{ill|Mappō|en|Mappō|qid=Q510060|lt=Mappō|1=Mappō}} - the Degenerate Age of Dharma *''miei-dō*'' (御影堂) – lit. "image hall". Building housing an image of the temple's founder, equivalent to a Zen sect's '''kaisan-dō'''.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''mi-dō'' (御堂) – a generic honorific term for a building which enshrines a sacred statue.<ref name="jaanus"/> *Miroku Nyorai (弥勒如来) – Japanese name of [[Maitreya]]. *''[[Wooden fish|mokugyō]]*'' – a large wooden bell used for religious ceremonies. *''mon'' (門) – a temple's gate, which can be named after its position ('''nandaimon''': lit. "great southern gate"), its structure ('''{{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|qid=Q3341490|lt=Nijūmon|1=Nijūmon}}''': "two storied gate"), a deity ('''Niōmon''': lit. "'''Nio''' gate"), or its use (''onarimon'': lit. "imperial visit gate", a gate reserved to the Emperor). The same gate can therefore be described using more than one term. For example, a '''Niōmon''' can at the same time be a '''nijūmon'''. *mukaikaramon (向唐門) – See '''karamon'''. *[[myokonin]] - pious followers of [[Jōdo Shinshū]]
==N== *''naijin'' (内陣) – the portion of a '''hon-dō''' (main hall) reserved to the deity, as opposed to the '''gejin''', open to worshipers.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''nandaimon*'' (南大門) – the main southern gate of a temple, in particular that at {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}'s Tōdai-ji.<ref name="jaanus"/> See also '''mon'''. *[[Buddhism in Japan#Asuka and Nara Periods|Nanto Rokushū]] (南都六宗) – six {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}} Buddhist sects, namely [[:en:Sanron|Sanron]] (三論), [[:en:Hossō|Hossō]] (法相), {{ill|Kegon|en|Kegon|qid=Q16652598|lt=Kegon|1=Kegon}} (華厳), [[Risshū (Buddhism)|Ritsu]] (律), [[:en:Kusha (Buddhism)|Kusha]] (倶舎), and [[:en:Jojitsu|Jōjitsu]] (成実).<ref name="kojien"/> *''nijūmon''* (二重門) – a two-storied gate with a roof surrounding the first floor.<ref name="jaanus"/> See also '''mon'''. *{{ill|Nianfo|en|Nianfo|qid=Q1315754|lt=Nianfo|1=Nianfo}} (念仏 or ねんぶつ) commonly seen in {{ill|Pure Land Buddhism|en|Pure Land Buddhism|qid=Q262244|lt=Pure Land Buddhism|1=Pure Land Buddhism}}. In the context of Pure Land practice, it generally refers to the repetition of the name of [[Amitābha]]. It is a translation of {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} ''{{ill|Buddhānusmṛti|en|Buddhānusmṛti|qid=Q22906947|lt={{IAST|buddhānusmṛti}}}}'' (or, "recollection of the {{ill|Buddhahood|en|Buddhahood|qid=Q56247062|lt=Buddha|1=Buddhahood}}") *''{{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|qid=Q539715|lt=Nio (Buddhism)|1=Nio (Buddhism)}}*'' (仁王 or 二王) – two muscular guardians standing at the sides of a gate to ward off evils spirits,<ref name="jaanus"/> one ('''Agyō''') with its mouth open to pronounce the sound "a", first letter of the {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|qid=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} alphabet and symbol of the beginning of all things, one ('''Ungyō''') with its mouth closed to pronounce the sound "un", last letter of the Sanskrit alphabet and symbol of the end of all things. *''{{ill|Niōmon|en|Niōmon|qid=Q3342330|lt=Niōmon|1=Niōmon}}*'' (仁王門 or 二王門) – a two-storied or high gate guarded by two wooden guardians called '''Niō'''.<ref name="jaanus"/> See also '''mon'''. *noborirō (登廊) – a covered stairway at Nara's [[:en:Hasedera|Hasedera]]. *Nyorai (如来) – Japanese term for '''tathagata'''. Someone who has reached {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|qid=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}.<ref name="jaanus"/> The most important Nyorai in Japan are '''Amida''', '''Yakushi''', '''Miroku''', and '''Dainichi'''.<ref name="jaanus"/>
==O== *''{{ill|Obon|en|Obon|qid=Q1154888|lt=Obon|1=Obon}}*'' (お盆) – three-day festival to honor one's {{ill|Ancestor|en|Ancestor|qid=Q402152|lt=Ancestor|1=Ancestor}}s. *''Okyō'' (お経) – see '''sūtra'''. * ''{{ill|Onigawara|en|Onigawara|qid=Q1895128|lt=Onigawara|1=Onigawara}}'' (鬼瓦) – lit. "ogre tile". A special tile carrying the face of an ogre installed at the corners of a temple's roof, originally to protect it from evil influences, nowadays as a decoration.<ref name="dea">DeAgostini</ref> There may not be an ogre's face. *''{{ill|Oizuru (garment)|en|Oizuru (garment)|qid=Q11580367|lt=Oizuru (garment)|1=Oizuru (garment)}}''
==Gallery: L to O== <gallery> Image:Mandala gross.jpg|A ''mandara'' Image:Swastika-pond-hasedera-kamakura-japan.jpg|A ''manji''-shaped pond at a Buddhist temple Image:Kyoto Toji Mieido C0973.jpg|''Miei-dō'' at Tō-ji Image:Chapin Mill Mokugyu Drum.JPG|A ''mokugyo'' Image:Horyu-ji02s3200.jpg|''Nandaimon'' at Hōryū-ji Image:Hasedera Noborirou.jpg|The ''noborirō'' at Nara's ''Hase-dera'' Image:Horyu-ji45s2s4500.jpg|''Nijūmon'' at [[Hōryū-ji]]. Image:Horyu-ji15s3200.jpg|A ''Niō'' at Hōryū-ji Image:Choshoji niomon.jpg|A ''niōmon'' Image:Bon Odori Dancer.jpg|Dancers at ''Obon'' Image:Nakayamadera hondo onigawara2048.jpg|''Onigawara'' </gallery>
==P== *{{ill|Pagoda|en|Pagoda|qid=Q199451|lt=Pagoda|1=Pagoda}}* – see '''stupa''' and ''tō''. *Posthumous name – see '''kaimyō'''.
==R== * {{nihongo|''Rakan''|, lit. "Silk Man"}} – An ''{{ill|Arhat|en|Arhat|qid=Q225932|lt=Arhat|1=Arhat}}'', a Buddhist saint. See also '''arakan'''. * ''rokujizō*'' (六地蔵) – series of six different statues of god '''Jizō''', often found along roads, where each is in charge of helping the beings of one of six worlds (of the Deva (天上), Ashura (阿修羅)，human beings (人間), hell (地獄), humans reincarnated as animals (畜生), and hungry spirits (餓鬼)).<ref name="jaanus"/> * ''{{ill|Rōmon|en|Rōmon|qid=Q3458957|lt=Rōmon|1=Rōmon}}'' (楼門) – a high gate with two floors, only one of which has usable space, surrounded by a balcony and topped by a roof.<ref name="jaanus"/> * ''[[Japanese calendar#Rokuyō|Rokuyō]]'' (), the six-day week of the {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhist|1=Buddhism}} {{ill|Calendar|en|Calendar|qid=Q12132|lt=Calendar|1=Calendar}}.
==S== *''{{ill|Saisen|en|Saisen|qid=Q988479|lt=saisen-bako|1=Saisen}}*'' (賽銭箱) – a box collecting the offerings (''saisen'') from worshipers, usually situated in front of an object of worship or a hall at temples and '''shrine'''s. *''sai-dō'' (斎堂) – the refectory at a Zen temple or monastery.<ref name="kojien"/> See also '''jiki-dō'''. *''{{ill|Sandō|en|Sandō|qid=Q667783|lt=Sandō|1=Sandō}}*'' (参道)- the approach leading from a '''torii''' to a '''shrine'''. The term is also used sometimes at Buddhist temples too. *''[[Buddhist temples in Japan#sangō|sangō]]'' (山号) – the so-called "mountain name" of a temple, always ending in "mount" (山), read ''-zan'' (e.g. "Tōei'''zan''' Kan'ei-ji Endon'in") or ''-san''. The other two are, in order, the '''jigō''' and the '''ingō'''. The use of the ''sangō'' came into fashion after the arrival of Zen Buddhism to Japan, therefore not all temples have one. *''san-in-jigō'' (山院寺号) – a temple's full name. *''[[sanmon]]*'' (三門 or 山門) – the gate in front of the '''butsuden'''.<ref name="jaanus"/> The name is short for {{nihongo|Sangedatsumon|三解脱門}}, lit. ''Gate of the three liberations''.<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three openings ({{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門}}) symbolize the three gates to enlightenment.<ref name="jaanus"/> Entering, one can free himself from three passions (貪 ''ton'', or greed, 瞋 ''shin'', or hatred, and 癡 ''chi'', or "foolishness"). See also '''mon'''. Its size depends on the temple's Rank. (See photos.) *''sanrō''* (山廊) – small buildings at the ends of a two-storied Zen gate containing the stairs to the second story. * {{ill|Satori|en|Satori|qid=Q584580|lt=Satori|1=Satori}} (悟り) – lit. "understanding". Japanese term for {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|qid=Q1137019|lt=Buddhist enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}. * {{nihongo|''{{ill|Segaki|en|Segaki|qid=Q7446184|lt=Segaki|1=Segaki}}''|, lit. "Feeding/Appeasing the Hungry Ghost"}} – A ritual of Japanese Buddhism, traditionally performed to stop the suffering of the {{nihongo|''[[Preta#Buddhism|Gaki]]''|, lit. "Hungry Ghosts"}}, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Jikininki|en|Jikininki|qid=Q2756347|lt=Jikininki|1=Jikininki}}''|, lit. "Man-eating Ghost/Oni"}} and {{nihongo|''Muenbotoke''|, lit. "Without Buddha"}}—the spirit of a departed mortal human with no living connections amongst the living; the dead who have no living relatives—ghosts tormented by insatiable 'hunger'. Alternatively, the ritual may be performed so-as to force them to return to their portion of hell, or-else keeps the spirits of the dead from falling into the realm of the ''Gaki'' in the first place. The ''Segaki'' may be performed at any time, but it is traditionally performed as part of the yearly ''{{ill|Obon|en|Obon|qid=Q1154888|lt=Ō-Bon Festival|1=Obon}}'' services in July to remember the dead and the ''Segaki'' ritual for offering alms to specifically ''Gaki'' &/or ''Muenbotoke'', but not for the spirits of one's ancestor. *''seisatsu*''(制札) – a signboard containing announcements and rules for worshipers. *''sekitō'' (石塔) – a stone '''pagoda''' ('''stupa''').<ref name="kojien"/> See also '''tō''' *Senbi Kannon (千臂観音) – See '''Senju Kannon'''. *Senju [[Kannon]] (千手観音) – the thousand-armed Goddess of Mercy. As a symbol of her mercy, the deity has also a thousand eyes (not present in statues for practical reasons) and is consequently often called Senju Sengen Kanjizai Bosatsu (千手千眼観自在菩薩) or simply Senju Sengen Kannon (千手千眼観音, thousand-armed, thousand-eyed Kannon).<ref name="jaanus"/> *''[[:en:Shaka Nyorai|Shaka Nyorai]]'' – Japanese name of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]], or Gautama Buddha. *''Shaka Sanzon'' (釈迦三尊) – the Shakyamuni Tathāgata Trinity, three statues representing Gautama '''Buddha''' "'''Shakyamuni Tathāgata'''" flanked by two other deities, which can be either [[:en:Manjusri|Monju Bosatsu]] and {{ill|Samantabhadra (Bodhisattva)|en|Samantabhadra (Bodhisattva)|qid=Q868306|lt=Fugen Bosatsu|1=Samantabhadra (Bodhisattva)}} or another pair.<ref name="kojien"/> *''[[shichidō garan]]*'' (七堂伽藍) – a double compound term literally meaning "seven halls" (七堂) and "(temple) buildings" (伽藍). What is counted in the group of seven buildings, or ''shichidō'', can vary greatly from temple to temple and from school to school. In practice, ''shichidō garan'' can also mean simply a large complex. **'''Nanto Rokushū''' and later non-Zen schools: The '''shichidō garan''' in this case includes a '''kon-dō''', a '''tō''', a '''kō-dō''', a '''shōrō''', a '''jiki-dō''', a '''sōbō''', and a '''kyōzō'''.<ref name="kojien"/> **Zen schools: A Zen '''shichidō garan''' includes a '''butsuden''' or '''butsu-dō''', a '''hattō''', a '''ku'in''', a '''sō-dō''', a '''sanmon''', a '''tōsu''' and a '''yokushitsu'''.<ref name="kojien"/> *''{{ill|Shimenawa|en|Shimenawa|qid=Q1887421|lt=Shimenawa|1=Shimenawa}}*'' (標縄・注連縄・七五三縄) – lit. "enclosing rope". A length of braided rice straw rope used for ritual purification often found at temples too. *''[[shinbutsu bunri]]'' (神仏分離) – the forbidding by law of the {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|qid=Q172904|lt=Syncretism|1=Syncretism}} of Shinto and Buddhism, and the effort to create a clear division between Shinto and Buddhism on one side, and Buddhist temples and Shinto '''shrine'''s on the other. *''{{ill|Shinbutsu kakuri|en|Shinbutsu kakuri|qid=Q3482168|lt=Shinbutsu kakuri|1=Shinbutsu kakuri}}'' (神仏隔離) − the tendency in medieval and early modern Japan to keep particular ''kami'' separate from any form or manifestation of Buddhism. *[[Shinbutsu-shūgō|Shinbutsu shūgō]] (神仏習合) – {{ill|Syncretism|en|Syncretism|qid=Q172904|lt=Syncretism|1=Syncretism}} of Buddhism and local religious beliefs, the normal state of things before the '''shinbutsu bunri'''. *Shinjin (信心) - true entrusting, shin (信) to mean truth, reality, sincerity; jin (心) means mind *Shi Tennō* (四天王) – the Four Heavenly Kings are the statues of four protector gods (the [[:en:Deva Kings|Deva Kings]]). *''{{ill|Shoin|en|Shoin|qid=Q3482442|lt=Shoin|1=Shoin}}'' (書院) – originally a study and a place for lectures on the '''sutra''' within a temple, later the term came to mean just a study.<ref name="kojien"/> *''[[shōrō]]'' (鐘楼)* – a temple's belfry, a building from which a bell is hung. *Sixteen Arhats – See '''Jūroku Rakan'''. *''sōbō'' (僧坊)* – The monks' living quarters in a non-Zen garan *''sō-dō*'' (僧堂) – Lit. "monk hall". A building dedicated to the practice of '''Zazen'''.<ref name="kojien"/> It used to be dedicated to all kinds of activities, from eating to sleeping, centered on zazen. *''sōmon*'' (総門) – the gate at the entrance of a temple.<ref name="kojien"/> It precedes the bigger and more important '''sanmon'''. See also '''mon'''. *''{{ill|Sōrin|en|Sōrin|qid=Q834370|lt=Sōrin|1=Sōrin}}*'' (相輪) – a spire reaching up from the center of the roof of some temple halls, tiered like a '''pagoda'''. *''sotoba*'' or sotōba (卒塔婆) – transliteration of the Sanskrit '''stupa'''. **A '''pagoda'''. Tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven nine, or thirteen). See also '''stupa'''. **Strips of wood left behind tombs during annual ceremonies ('''tsuizen''') symbolizing a '''stupa'''.<ref name="kojien"/> The upper part is segmented like a '''pagoda''' and carries Sanskrit inscriptions, '''sutras''', and the '''kaimyō''' ({{ill|Posthumous name|en|Posthumous name|qid=Q696100|lt=Posthumous name|1=Posthumous name}}) of the deceased. In present-day Japanese, ''sotoba'' usually has the latter meaning. *''[[stupa]]'' – in origin a vessel for Buddha's relics, later also a receptacle for scriptures and other relics. Its shape changed in the Far East under the influence of the Chinese watchtower to form tower-like structures like the '''buttō''', the '''gorintō''', the '''hōkyōintō''', the '''sekitō''', the '''tō''', or the much simpler wooden stick-style '''sotoba'''.<ref name="jaanus"/> *{{ill|Sutra|en|Sutra|qid=Q209680|lt=Sutra|1=Sutra}} – the sacred scriptures of Buddhism.
==Gallery: P to S== <gallery> File:Yakushiji-MF007-0069.jpg|Pagoda at [[Yakushi-ji]] in Nara File:Kanakura no Rokujizou 01.jpg|The ''rokujizō'' File:Udo_Jingu_Roumon.jpg|A ''rōmon'' File:Saisen-honkakuji-kamakura.jpg|A ''saisen-bako'', or offertory box File:Stone stairway Kiyomizu-dera.JPG|[[Kiyomizu-dera]]'s ''sandō'' File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|A high Rank, five-bay ''sanmon'' at [[Chion-in]]. Note the ''sanrō''. File:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg|A middleRank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at {{ill|Myōtsū-ji|en|Myōtsū-ji|qid=Q3331935|lt=Myōtsū-ji|1=Myōtsū-ji}} File:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg|A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} File:Tofukiji-Sanro.jpg|The ''sanrō'' of Tōfuku-ji's ''sanmon''. (See also the ''sanmon'''s photo above.) File:Seisatsu.jpg|A ''seisatsu'' File:Fujitoji 06.JPG|A ''sekitō'', or stone pagoda File:Toshodaiji Nara Nara pref04s5s4290.jpg|{{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|qid=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji|1=Tōshōdai-ji}}'s ''shichidō garan'' (left to right, the ''kondō'', the ''kōdō'', and the ''korō'')
File:Choshjoji Statue of Nichiren.jpg|Statue of Nichiren guarded by the Shi Tennō File:Eikando Somon.jpg|{{ill|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|en|Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji|qid=Q2380134|lt=Zenrin-ji|1=Eikan-dō Zenrin-ji}}'s ''sōmon'' File:Negoroji03s3200.jpg|A large ''sōrin'' (metal spire) on top of [[Negoro-ji]]'s ''tahōto'' File:Saifukuji09s3872.jpg|[[Saifuku-ji]]'s ''shoin'' File:Saidaiji-M6662.jpg|[[Saidai-ji]]'s ''shōrō'' (early type) File:Todaiji shoro.jpg|Tōdai-ji's ''shōrō'' (later type) File:Sotoba At Kurosawa-Akira's-grave.jpg|Some ''sotoba'' </gallery>
==T== *''{{visible anchor|tatchū}}'' (塔頭 or 塔中) **In Zen temples, a building containing a pagoda enshrining the ashes of an important priest stands.<ref name="jaanus"/> **Later, it became a subsidiary temple or a minor temple depending from a larger one.<ref name="jaanus"/> **Finally, it became also subsidiary temple being the family temple ('''bodaiji''') of an important family.<ref name="jaanus"/> *[[tahōtō]]* (多宝塔) – a two-storied '''{{ill|Pagoda|en|Pagoda|qid=Q199451|lt=Pagoda|1=Pagoda}}''' with a ground floor having a dome-shaped ceiling and a square pent roof, a round second floor and square roofs.<ref name="jaanus"/> *[[:en:Tariki (Buddhism)|tariki]] (他力) - "other power", "outside help" *''{{ill|Chōzuya|en|Chōzuya|qid=Q1069667|lt=Chōzuya|1=Chōzuya}}*'' (手水舎) – a fountain near the entrance of a '''shrine''' and a temple where worshipers can cleanse their hands and mouths before worship.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''{{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}'' – term used with {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|qid=Q845945|lt=shrine|1=Shinto shrine}} to mirror in English the distinction between the Japanese words '''tera''' and ''jinja''. The three most common temple name endings are: **-ji (寺) – most common suffix in temple names meaning simply "temple", as for example in [[Nanzen-ji]]. **-in (院) – less common and normally used for minor temples or subtemples, as for example [[Meigetsu-in]]. ** – dera (寺) – alternate reading of -ji, and identical in meaning. Used occasionally, for example in [[:en:Kiyomizudera|Kiyomizudera]] *''Ten'' (天) - an Indian god of non Buddhist origin, for example [[Benzaiten]], {{ill|Brahma|en|Brahma|qid=Q11389|lt=Bonten|1=Brahma}}, {{ill|Indra|en|Indra|qid=Q128335|lt=Taishakuten|1=Indra}}, and [[:en:Ganesh|Kankiten]].<ref name="jaanus"/> * ''tera'' (寺) – see ''temple''. *''[[Danka system|terauke]]'' – document granted by temples to their '''danka''' during the Edo period to certify they were not Christian.<ref name="kojien"/> *''tesaki*'' (手先) – Term used to count the roof-supporting brackets (''tokyō'' (斗きょう)) projecting from a temple's wall, usually composed of two steps (''futatesaki'' (二手先）)) or three (''mitesaki'' 三津手先).<ref name="jaanus"/> *''[[:en:Tokyō|Tokyō]]'' (斗きょう) – see ''tesaki''. *''[[torii]]*'' (鳥居)- the iconic Shinto gate at the entrance of a sacred area, usually, but not always, a '''shrine''. Shrines of various size can be found next to, or inside temples. *''{{ill|Stone lantern|en|Stone lantern|qid=Q12012294|lt=Stone lantern|1=Stone lantern}}*'' (灯籠) – a lantern at a '''shrine''' or {{ill|Buddhist temples in Japan|en|Buddhist temples in Japan|qid=Q3141900|lt=Buddhist temple|1=Buddhist temples in Japan}}. Some of its forms are influenced by the '''gorintō'''. *''[[Japanese pagoda|Tō]]'' (塔) **Generic word for tower, and for pagoda (an evolution of the '''stupa''') in particular, and. After reaching China, the '''stupa''' evolved into a tower with an odd number of tiers (three, five, seven, nine, thirteen), excepted the '''tahōtō''', which has two.<ref name="jaanus"/> **The word is used together as a suffix of a numeral indicating the number of a pagoda's tiers (three tiers= san-jū-no-tō, five tiers= go-jū-no-tō, seven tiers = nana-jū-no-tō, etc.). *''tōsu*'' or ''tōshi'' (東司) – a Zen monastery's toilet.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''tsuizen'' (追善) – Buddhist ceremony held on the anniversary of someone's death.<ref name="kojien"/> *[[Twelve Heavenly Generals]] – see ''Jūni Shinshō''
==U== *''Ungyō'' (吽形) – A statue (of a '''Niō''', '''komainu''', etc.) with its mouth closed to pronounce the sound "un", last letter of the Sanskrit alphabet and symbol of the end of all things. See also '''Agyō'''.
==V== *{{ill|Vairocana|en|Vairocana|qid=Q239847|lt=Vairocana|1=Vairocana}} – Vairocana is a '''buddha''' who is the embodiment of [[:en:Dharmakaya|Dharmakaya]], and who therefore can be seen as the universal aspect of the historical Gautama Buddha. Called '''Dainichi Nyorai''' in Japanese.
==Y== *''Yakushi Nyorai'' (薬師如来) – Japanese name of {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Bhaisajyaguru|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}, the Buddha of healing. *''Yakushi-dō*'' (薬師堂) – a building that enshrines a statue of [[:en:Yakushi|Yakushi]] Nyorai.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''yodarekake '' (涎掛け) – votive bibs found on '''Jizō''' and statues of foxes, sacred to ''kami'' {{ill|Inari Ōkami|en|Inari Ōkami|qid=Q719665|lt=Inari|1=Inari Ōkami}}. *''yokushitsu*'' (浴室) – a monastery's bathroom.<ref name="jaanus"/> *''yosemune-zukuri*'' (寄棟造) – A {{ill|Hip roof|en|Hip roof|qid=Q162034|lt=hipped roof|1=Hip roof}} where the front and back are trapezoidal and the sides triangular in shape. A classic example is [[Tōdai-ji]]'s Daibutsuden.
==Z== * ''{{ill|Zazen|en|Zazen|qid=Q167894|lt=Zazen|1=Zazen}}*'' (座禅) – transliteration of the Sanskrit '''dhyāna'''.<ref name="kojien"/> The Zen performed sitting just down and meditating.<ref name="kojien"/> Mainly an activity of Zen schools. * ''{{ill|Zen|en|Zen|qid=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}}'' (禅) – Mahayana school of Buddhism that had great influence and power in Japan through the Five Mountain System (see '''Gozan seido'''). It is itself divided in three sub-schools, [[Sōtō]] ({{lang|ja|曹洞}}), {{ill|Rinzai school|en|Rinzai school|qid=Q639625|lt=Rinzai school|1=Rinzai school}} ({{lang|ja|臨済}}), and {{ill|Ōbaku|en|Ōbaku|qid=Q384728|lt=Ōbaku|1=Ōbaku}} ({{lang|ja|黃檗}}). * {{nihongo|''{{ill|Kalyāṇa-mittatā|en|Kalyāṇa-mittatā|qid=Q6355077|lt=Zenchishiki|1=Kalyāṇa-mittatā}}''|, lit. "Good Friend(ship)"}} – The Japanese ''Shinto''-''Buddhist'' name for the Buddhist concept of ''Kalyāṇa-mittatā''; similar to a [[:en:Death doula|Death doula]]. * ''{{ill|Zendō|en|Zendō|qid=Q642574|lt=Zendō|1=Zendō}}*'' (禅堂) – lit. "hall of Zen".<ref name="jaanus"/> The building where monks practice '''zazen''', and one of the main structures of a Zen '''garan'''.<ref name="jaanus"/> * ''Zokumyō'' (俗名) – the name a Buddhist priest had before taking his vows<ref name="kojien"/>
== Gallery: T to Z == <gallery> File:Kongosanmaiin Tahoto.JPG|A ''tahōtō'' (''nijū-no-tō'') File:Hogonji18s3200.jpg|A three-tiered ''-to'' (''sanjū-no-tō'') File:Mii-dera Otsu Shiga pref27n4592.jpg|{{ill|Mii-dera|en|Mii-dera|qid=Q1756307|lt=Mii-dera|1=Mii-dera}}'s ''temizuya'' File:Komyo-ji_Kamakura_Belltower_futatesaki.jpg|Two-tiered brackets (futatesaki ''tokyō''). Enlarge to see numbers. File:Oyake-ji_01.jpg|A ''torii'' on a temple's (Oyake-ji) ''sandō'' File:Laterne japangarten.JPG|A ''tōrō'' File:Toufuku-ji tousu.JPG|A ''tōsu'' File:Nikko Toshogu Nio M3047.jpg|An example of ''ungyō'' (in this case, a '''Niō''') File:Jōdo-ji, Yakushi Hall 001.jpg|{{ill|Jōdo-ji (Ono)|en|Jōdo-ji (Ono)|qid=Q3077393|lt=Jōdo-ji|1=Jōdo-ji (Ono)}}'s ''yakushi-dō'' File:Myoshinji-DSC1246.jpg|A ''yokushitsu'' File:TofukujiZendo.jpg|Tōfuku-ji's ''zen-dō'' File:Tōdai-ji Kon-dō.jpg|An example of ''yosemune-zukuri'' </gallery>

Iwanami Shoten, 1999–2001. * [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System] Dictionary of Japanese Architectural and Art Historical Terminology accessed on April 2, 2009 * {{cite book | last = Smyers, Karen Ann | author-link = Karen Ann Smyers | title = The Fox and the Jewel: Shared and Private Meanings in Contemporary Japanese Inari Worship | publisher = University of Hawaii Press | year = 1999 | location = Honolulu | isbn = 0-8248-2102-5 | oclc = 231775156 }}
